name: Number Systems, Powers and Scientific Notation
whatIsIt: Numbers come in nested families. NATURAL: 1, 2, 3… WHOLE: add 0. INTEGERS: add negatives. RATIONAL: fractions/decimals that stop or repeat. IRRATIONAL: never-ending, non-repeating decimals like √2 and π. REAL: rationals + irrationals together. POWERS (exponents) write repeated multiply: 10³ = 1000. SCIENTIFIC NOTATION writes huge or tiny numbers neatly: 3,00,000 = 3 × 10⁵.
whyNeeded: Knowing which family a number belongs to, and using powers and scientific notation, is essential for science (distances, sizes of atoms), computing (data sizes), and all higher maths.
realLifeUses: Writing the speed of light or a population as 3 × 10⁸
Sizes of tiny things like a virus (2 × 10⁻⁷ m)
π and √2 appear in circles and diagonals (irrational)

vocabulary.meaning: A decimal that never ends and never repeats, like √2 or π.
A number as (1–10) × a power of 10, e.g. 4500 = 4.5 × 10³.

story.title: The Big and the Tiny
story.text: Sir writes 300000 on the board. 'Write it shorter,' he says. Diya counts five zeros: 3 followed by 5 zeros is 3 × 10⁵. 'And is √2 rational?' Sir asks. Diya recalls: √2 = 1.41421… never ends, never repeats. 'It's irrational!' Sir nods — 'real numbers hold both.'
story.answerInStory: 3 × 10⁵ — 3 with the decimal point moved 5 places.

visual.caption: Naturals ⊂ Wholes ⊂ Integers ⊂ Rationals ⊂ Reals; irrationals (√2, π) fill the gaps to make the reals.

teachingGallery.title: Families, powers, big/small
teachingGallery.note: Irrationals never stop/repeat; powers pack repeated ×10.

examples.caption: Each power of 10 adds a zero.
√2 sits between 1 and 2, never exactly a fraction.
Move the point, count the places.

standardMethod.summary: Classify a number by its family; use exponents for repeated multiplication; write big/small numbers as (1–10) × a power of 10.
steps: Family: whole? has a minus? a fraction/terminating-or-repeating decimal (rational)? never-ending non-repeating (irrational)? All of these are REAL.
Exponent: aⁿ means multiply a by itself n times (2⁵ = 32).
Scientific notation: move the decimal so one non-zero digit is in front, and count the places as the power of 10.
Big number → positive power; tiny number → negative power (0.0003 = 3 × 10⁻⁴).
Put one digit before the point: 4.5
3 is a whole number, which is rational.
So √9 is rational (only non-perfect-square roots like √2 are irrational).

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking every square root is irrational (calling √9 irrational).
commonMistakes.fix: Roots of PERFECT squares are rational (√9 = 3). Only roots like √2, √3, √5 (non-perfect) are irrational.
An exponent is REPEATED multiplication of the base, not multiplication by the exponent. 10³ = 1000.

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up how to write a big number (like 3,00,000) as 3 × 10⁵ by counting the zeros, and explain why √2 and π are irrational (they never end or repeat) while √9 is rational (= 3).

revisionCard.summary: Families nest: Naturals ⊂ Wholes ⊂ Integers ⊂ Rationals ⊂ Reals; irrationals (√2, π: never end/repeat) fill the reals. Exponent aⁿ = repeated multiply (10³ = 1000, not 30). Scientific notation = (1–10) × power of 10; big → +power, tiny → −power. Perfect-square roots are rational.

meta.curriculum: CBSE/NCERT Class 7-9 (number systems, exponents, scientific notation)

rememberIt.hook: The number family: naturals, whole, integers, rationals, reals
rememberIt.unpack: Naturals 1,2,3; add 0 -> whole; add negatives -> integers; add fractions -> rationals; add irrationals -> reals.

studentQuestions.a: Whole numbers are 0,1,2,3... Integers add the negatives: ...-2,-1,0,1,2...
They are rational numbers (any p/q). With irrationals like sqrt 2 they make the reals.
